---
title: "BSAD 8310: Business Forecasting"
subtitle: "Course Syllabus — Fall 2026"
format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: false
    self-contained: true
---

---

## Course Information

| | |
|---|---|
| **Course** | BSAD 8310: Business Forecasting |
| **Credits** | 3 |
| **Term** | Fall 2026 |
| **Meeting Time** | Thursdays, 6:00 PM – 8:40 PM |
| **Location** | TBD |
| **Instructor** | TBD |
| **Email** | TBD |
| **Office Hours** | TBD |
| **Course Website** | TBD |

---

## Course Description

This course develops practical forecasting skills for graduate students in business,
economics, and data science. Students learn to apply a broad suite of methods —
from classical time series models to machine learning and Bayesian inference — to
real business forecasting problems. Emphasis is on implementation in Python,
interpretation of model outputs for business decision-making, and critical comparison
of methods across different forecasting contexts.

---

## Learning Objectives

By the end of this course, students will be able to:

1. Apply exponential smoothing, ARIMA, and VAR models to univariate and multivariate
   business time series and evaluate their forecast accuracy using walk-forward cross-validation.
2. Build Generalized Additive Models (GAMs) and use Prophet to decompose trend,
   seasonality, and external regressors.
3. Engineer features from time series data and apply decision trees, random forests,
   and gradient boosting to tabular forecasting problems.
4. Design, train, and evaluate feedforward networks, CNNs, LSTMs, and Transformer
   encoders for sequence forecasting using PyTorch.
5. Apply Bayesian inference to specify priors, compute posteriors, and generate
   calibrated probabilistic forecasts using PyMC.
6. Communicate model results and business recommendations to both technical and
   non-technical audiences.

---

## Prerequisites

Graduate standing. Familiarity with basic statistics (regression, hypothesis testing)
and introductory Python programming is assumed. No prior knowledge of machine learning
or time series analysis is required.

---

## Required Software

All coursework uses Python. The following packages are required:

```
pandas, numpy, matplotlib, statsmodels, prophet, pygam,
scikit-learn, xgboost, torch, pymc, arviz
```

Students should have a working Python 3.10+ environment with these packages installed
before the first class. Installation instructions and a test script are provided in
the course repository.

---

## Course Materials

All lecture slides (Beamer PDF and interactive RevealJS), homework assignments, and
data files are distributed through the course repository. There is no required textbook.
Selected readings are listed under individual lectures below.

---

## Course Schedule

Dates are subject to adjustment. Any changes will be announced in class and posted to
the course site.

| Week | Date | Topic | Homework |
|------|------|-------|----------|
| 1  | Aug 27 | **L01:** Introduction, Exponential Smoothing & Forecast Evaluation | |
| 2  | Sep 3  | **L02:** ARIMA, VAR & Multivariate Models | HW1 assigned |
| 3  | Sep 10 | **L03:** Generalized Additive Models | |
| 4  | Sep 17 | **L04:** Decision Trees | HW1 due; HW2 assigned; **project groups due** |
| 5  | Sep 24 | **L05:** Random Forests | |
| 6  | Oct 1  | **L06:** Boosted Trees & Regularization | HW2 due; HW3 assigned |
| 7  | Oct 8  | **L07:** Introduction to Neural Networks | |
| 8  | Oct 15 | **L08:** CNN Architectures | HW3 due; HW4 assigned |
| —  | Oct 19–20 | *Fall Break — No Class* | |
| 9  | Oct 22 | **L09:** RNNs, LSTMs & Transformers | **Project proposal due; HW5 assigned** |
| 10 | Oct 29 | **L10:** Bayesian Statistics I — Foundations | HW4 due |
| 11 | Nov 5  | **L11:** Bayesian Statistics II — Time Series & Hierarchical | |
| 12 | Nov 12 | **L12:** Bayesian Statistics III — Bayesian Linear Regression | |
| 13 | Nov 19 | Project work session / consultations | HW5 due |
| —  | Nov 26 | *Thanksgiving Break — No Class* | |
| 14 | Dec 3  | Project work session / consultations | |
| 15 | Dec 10 | Last class / project consultations | |
| —  | Dec 15 | **Final Project Report Due** (Finals Week) | |

*Note: Homework assignments HW7 and HW8 cover Bayesian methods and may be assigned
as standalone work or incorporated into the final project at the instructor's discretion.*

---

## Assignments and Grading

### Homework Assignments

There are **5 graded homework assignments** (HW1–HW5). Each is completed as a
Quarto (`.qmd`) document rendered to HTML and submitted through the course repository.

Each homework includes:
- Starter code and data loading
- Analytical questions with Python implementation
- Written interpretation questions requiring your own reasoning
- A short `REPORT.md` with your business recommendation

Homework is designed to be completed with the assistance of an AI coding tool (see
AI Use Policy below). Questions emphasize interpretation, judgment, and business
reasoning — tasks where AI assistance is a tool, not a substitute for understanding.

### Final Project

The final project is completed in **groups of 2–3 students**. Groups are formed by
Week 4 and submitted to the instructor for approval.

**Dataset requirements:** Each group selects a real business dataset of their choosing.
The dataset must:
- Be a time series or convertible to one (e.g., daily, weekly, or monthly observations)
- Have at least 100 time periods
- Have a clear, specific business forecasting question (e.g., "forecast weekly demand
  for SKU X to support inventory decisions" — not just "predict a number")

**Required content:** The final report must:
1. Fit at least **3 methods from at least 2 different course parts** (e.g., one
   classical time series method, one tree-based method, and one neural network or
   Bayesian model)
2. Use **walk-forward cross-validation** for all model comparisons
3. Include a **business recommendation section** written for a non-technical
   decision-maker — actionable, jargon-free, and grounded in your results
4. Include a **methods reflection** that explains which model performed best and
   connects that result to characteristics of your specific dataset

**Deliverables:**

| Deliverable | Due Date | Description |
|-------------|----------|-------------|
| Group formation | Sep 17 (Week 4) | Submit group members to instructor |
| Project proposal | Nov 19 (Week 13) | 1–2 pages: dataset, business question, planned methods, preliminary EDA |
| Final report | Dec 15 (Finals Week) | Full written report (details below) |

**Final report format:** Submit as a rendered Quarto HTML file (`.qmd` + `.html`)
through the course repository. The report should include all code, outputs, and
written analysis in a single self-contained document. There is no page limit, but
conciseness is valued: a well-organized 15–20 page report is better than a
disorganized 40-page one.

The grading rubric will be distributed by Week 6.

### Grading Breakdown

| Component | Weight |
|-----------|--------|
| Homework (HW1–HW5) | TBD |
| Final Project | TBD |
| **Total** | **100%** |

### Grading Scale

| Grade | Range |
|-------|-------|
| A | 90–100% |
| B | 80–89% |
| C | 70–79% |
| D | 60–69% |
| F | Below 60% |

---

## AI Use Policy

This course explicitly permits and encourages the use of AI coding assistants
(such as Codex, GitHub Copilot, or ChatGPT) for homework assignments.
The course infrastructure is designed around this: each assignment includes a
required initial prompt and a prompt budget (maximum number of AI interactions per assignment).

**You are responsible for your results.** AI tools help write code; you are
responsible for understanding and explaining the output. Grading emphasizes:

- Correct interpretation of model results in business language
- Reasoned justification of methodological choices
- Diagnosing unexpected or poor results rather than accepting them uncritically
- Written reflection questions that require genuine understanding

**Prompt logging is required.** Students must maintain a `PROMPT_LOG.jsonl` file
recording every AI interaction for each assignment. Submissions without a complete
prompt log will receive a grade deduction.

**Written responses must be your own.** Analytical interpretation and business
recommendation sections must be written by you, not generated by AI. Using AI
to write your interpretations defeats the purpose of the assignment and
violates the spirit (though not the letter) of this policy.

---

## Online and In-Person Attendance

This course is offered simultaneously in-person and fully online. All lecture slides
are designed to be self-contained: an online student watching asynchronously can
follow without attending the live session.

In-person attendance is encouraged but not graded. Recordings of class sessions
will be made available within 48 hours of each meeting when possible.

---

## Late Work Policy

Homework submitted late will be penalized **10% per calendar day** up to a maximum
of 5 days. Homework submitted more than 5 days late will receive a grade of zero.
Extensions may be granted for documented emergencies; contact the instructor
before the deadline.

---

## Academic Integrity

All work submitted must represent your own understanding and analysis. The AI use
policy above describes the boundaries for AI assistance. Beyond AI tools:

- Do not share code or written responses with other students.
- Do not submit another student's work as your own.
- Citations are required for any external sources used in written sections.

Violations will be handled according to UNO's Academic Integrity Policy.
See [TBD: link to UNO Academic Integrity Policy].

---

## Accessibility and Accommodations

Students with disabilities who require accommodations should contact the
UNO Accessibility Services Center and provide documentation to the instructor
within the first two weeks of the semester. The instructor is committed to
providing an accessible learning environment for all students.

---

## Course Communication

The instructor will respond to email within 48 business hours. For questions about
homework, please post to the course discussion board so that all students benefit
from the answer. For personal or grade-related matters, email directly.

---

## A Note on Difficulty and Pacing

This course covers a wide range of methods — from classical statistics to deep
learning to Bayesian inference. Some weeks will be more familiar to you than others
depending on your background. Students with a data science background may find
Parts 1–2 straightforward and Part 4 (Bayesian) more challenging. Economics students
may find the reverse. MBA students will find Parts 1–2 most accessible and should
expect to invest extra time in Parts 3–4.

The methods build on each other. Falling behind in one week makes the next harder.
Use office hours early and often.

---

*Syllabus subject to change. Any modifications will be announced in class and
posted to the course website with at least one week's notice.*
